cases.history_present_illness: Patient presents with acute onset of right-sided hemiparesis and dysarthria starting 45 minutes prior to arrival. Family notes she was eating breakfast when she dropped her fork. No loss of consciousness reported. History of atrial fibrillation, not currently on anticoagulation due to recent GI bleed.
cases.assessment_context: High suspicion for acute ischemic stroke within thrombolytic window. Contraindication assessment for lytics is critical given history of GI bleed.
cases.clinical_question: What specific contraindications must be ruled out before considering thrombolysis in this patient?

metadata.organization: American Heart Association/American Stroke Association
metadata.guideline_title: Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update.

T.excerpt_text:
• IV alteplase is recommended for eligible patients within 4.5 hours of symptom onset
• Blood pressure should be controlled to <185/110 mmHg before thrombolysis
• Contraindications include: recent major surgery, active bleeding, INR >1.7, platelets <100,000
• Mechanical thrombectomy is recommended for large vessel occlusion within 24 hours in select patients
• CTA or MRA should be obtained to assess for LVO
• NIHSS ≥6 generally supports intervention consideration
safety_guardrails: Assessment of bleeding risk is mandatory before thrombolysis
Obtain baseline labs including glucose, creatinine, INR, and platelet count
Confirm time of symptom onset or last known well
Screen for contraindications systematically
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors
exclusions_or_cautions: Active internal bleeding
Recent intracranial surgery or serious head trauma within 3 months
History of intracranial hemorrhage
Uncontrolled hypertension at time of treatment

cases.history_present_illness: Admitted 2 days ago for UTI, now with worsening confusion and new fever spike. Nursing staff reports patient is picking at lines and oriented only to self. Urine culture from admission showed E. coli sensitive to Ceftriaxone, which he is receiving.
cases.assessment_context: Deterioration despite antibiotics suggesting either resistance, wrong source, or progression to severe sepsis. Renal function has worsened significantly from baseline.
cases.clinical_question: What antibiotic adjustments or diagnostic steps are indicated given the renal decline and worsening clinical status?

metadata.organization: Surviving Sepsis Campaign
metadata.guideline_title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021.

T.excerpt_text:
• Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition
• Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not delay administration
• Administer 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
• Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor
• Add vasopressin as second agent if MAP target not achieved
• Epinephrine may be added to norepinephrine
safety_guardrails: Reassess volume status and tissue perfusion frequently
Avoid fluid overload in patients with adequate perfusion
Consider renal dose adjustments for antibiotics
Monitor for end-organ dysfunction
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Patient reports worst headache of life starting suddenly 2 hours ago. Associated with photophobia and nausea. No history of migraines. Reports 'feeling weird' before onset.
imaging: CT Head non-contrast: Negative for acute bleed
cases.assessment_context: Clinical presentation concerns for SAH despite negative non-contrast CT. Need to decide on LP vs further imaging noting contrast allergy.
cases.clinical_question: What is the appropriate next diagnostic step for suspected SAH after a negative CT head within 6 hours?

metadata.organization: American College of Emergency Physicians
metadata.guideline_title: Clinical Policy: Critical Issues in the Evaluation and Management of Adult Patients With Acute Headache
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Clinical Policy: Critical Issues in the Evaluation and Management of Adult Patients With Acute Headache.

T.excerpt_text:
• Sudden onset worst headache of life requires SAH workup
• Fever with headache requires evaluation for meningitis
• Papilledema suggests elevated ICP requiring imaging
• Obtain CT head for red flag headaches
• Consider LP if SAH suspected with negative CT
• MRI for focal neurological signs
safety_guardrails: Do not attribute new severe headache to tension/migraine without evaluation
Age >50 with new headache type requires further workup
Document neurological examination thoroughly
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Brought in by EMS after witnessed tonic-clonic seizure lasting 3 minutes. Patient is post-ictal but arousable. Wife reports he missed his seizure meds for 3 days. Also complains of recent cough.
cases.assessment_context: Seizure likely due to medication non-compliance, but fever and CXR suggest concurrent pneumonia. Need to manage seizure threshold while treating infection.
cases.clinical_question: Which antibiotics should be avoided in this patient due to the risk of lowering seizure threshold?

metadata.organization: Infectious Diseases Society of America/American Thoracic Society
metadata.guideline_title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia.

T.excerpt_text:
• Amoxicillin or doxycycline for healthy outpatients without comorbidities
• Respiratory fluoroquinolone OR beta-lactam + macrolide for outpatients with comorbidities
• Consider local antibiotic resistance patterns
• Non-ICU: Beta-lactam (ampicillin-sulbactam, ceftriaxone, cefotaxime) + macrolide OR respiratory fluoroquinolone
• ICU: Beta-lactam + macrolide or beta-lactam + fluoroquinolone
• Add MRSA coverage if risk factors present
safety_guardrails: Assess for drug allergies before prescribing
Consider QT prolongation risk with fluoroquinolones and macrolides
Adjust doses for renal impairment
Evaluate for aspiration risk
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Admitted for Cellulitis of LLE, developed acute SOB on day 2. Chest pain is pleuritic. On prophylactic heparin.
cases.assessment_context: High suspicion for PE despite negative leg doppler. Renal function is impaired (CKD/AKI). Contrast CT requires consideration of renal risk vs benefit.

metadata.organization: Infectious Diseases Society of America
metadata.guideline_title: Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections.

T.excerpt_text:
• Purulent: abscess, furuncle, carbuncle
• Non-purulent: cellulitis, erysipelas
• Necrotizing soft tissue infection is a surgical emergency
• I&D is primary treatment for purulent SSTI
• Antibiotics covering MRSA for purulent infections in high-prevalence areas
• Non-purulent cellulitis: beta-lactam (cephalexin, dicloxacillin)
safety_guardrails: Assess for signs of necrotizing infection (pain out of proportion, crepitus)
Mark borders of cellulitis to monitor progression
Check for systemic toxicity requiring IV antibiotics
Surgical debridement for necrotizing fasciitis
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.assessment_context: Traumatic intracranial hemorrhage in patient on DOAC. Need to address anticoagulation reversal. Renal function is poor.
cases.clinical_question: What is the appropriate reversal strategy for Apixaban in the setting of acute intracranial hemorrhage?

metadata.organization: American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association/ACCP/HRS
metadata.guideline_title: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation.

T.excerpt_text:
• Calculate CHA2DS2-VASc score to assess stroke risk
• DOACs preferred over warfarin for most patients
• Anticoagulation recommended for CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women)
• Beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers for rate control
• Target resting heart rate <110 bpm for lenient control
• Avoid AV nodal blocking agents if pre-excitation present
safety_guardrails: Screen for contraindications to anticoagulation
Assess renal function for DOAC dosing
Check for drug interactions with anticoagulants
Evaluate for valvular AF which requires warfarin
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.chief_complaint: Severe sore throat and difficulty swallowing
cases.history_present_illness: 3 days of sore throat, fever, now with trismus and 'hot potato' voice. Difficulty handling secretions. No cough.
cases.assessment_context: Suspicion for peritonsillar abscess. Penicillin allergy noted. Airway is patent currently but concerning.
cases.clinical_question: What antibiotic options cover the likely pathogens for peritonsillar abscess considering the patient's allergy?

metadata.organization: ENT UK
metadata.guideline_title: Adult Acute Severe Sore Throat Management Guideline
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Adult Acute Severe Sore Throat Management Guideline.

T.excerpt_text:
• Use Centor or FeverPAIN score to assess likelihood of strep
• RADT or throat culture if moderate-high likelihood
• Assess for peritonsillar abscess: uvular deviation, trismus, hot potato voice
• Strep pharyngitis: Penicillin VK or amoxicillin x 10 days
• Symptomatic: NSAIDs, acetaminophen, throat lozenges
• Peritonsillar abscess: I&D or needle aspiration + IV antibiotics
safety_guardrails: Avoid amoxicillin if EBV suspected (rash risk)
Watch for airway compromise in severe cases
Refer persistent or bilateral peritonsillar swelling urgently
Consider deep space neck infection if toxic appearing
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: POD 2 from hip replacement. Nurse reports patient is agitated, hallucinating, and pulling at IVs. Last pain med dose 4 hours ago.
cases.assessment_context: Delirium vs Serotonin Syndrome vs Pain. High Serotonergic load (Citalopram + Sertraline + Oxycodone). QTc is prolonged.
cases.clinical_question: What medication adjustments are needed to address the serotonin load and QT prolongation risk?

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Delirium: prevention, diagnosis and management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Delirium: prevention, diagnosis and management.

T.excerpt_text:
• Use CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) for diagnosis
• Identify and treat underlying cause (infection, medications, metabolic)
• Review medication list for deliriogenic drugs
• Non-pharmacological interventions first: reorientation, sleep hygiene
• Avoid physical restraints when possible
• Low-dose haloperidol only for severe agitation endangering patient/staff
safety_guardrails: Avoid benzodiazepines (except alcohol/benzo withdrawal)
Minimize anticholinergic medications
Fall precautions and close monitoring
Involve family for reorientation support
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Painless loss of vision in right eye like a 'curtain coming down'. Occurred 1 hour ago. No trauma.
cases.assessment_context: Concern for embolic phenomenon (CRAO) vs retinal detachment. IVDU history raises concern for endocarditis seeding.
cases.clinical_question: What systemic evaluation is warranted given the patient's social history and ocular presentation?

metadata.organization: European Society of Cardiology
metadata.guideline_title: ESC Guidelines for the Management of Endocarditis
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from ESC Guidelines for the Management of Endocarditis.

T.excerpt_text:
• Modified Duke criteria for diagnosis
• Obtain 3 sets of blood cultures from separate sites before antibiotics
• TTE first; TEE if TTE negative or high suspicion
• Empiric: Vancomycin + ceftriaxone pending cultures
• Native valve staph: Oxacillin (MSSA) or vancomycin (MRSA) x 6 weeks
• Prosthetic valve: Longer duration, add rifampin and gentamicin
safety_guardrails: Surgical intervention for HF, abscess, or persistent bacteremia
Screen for stroke before surgery
Monitor for embolic events
Dental evaluation and treatment as appropriate
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: European Society of Cardiology
metadata.guideline_title: ESC Guidelines for the Management of Endocarditis
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from ESC Guidelines for the Management of Endocarditis.

cases.history_present_illness: Daughter reports patient has been weak for 3 days and had melena today. Patient is dizzy upon standing.
cases.assessment_context: Upper GI bleed with hemodynamic instability. Anticoagulated on Rivaroxaban with acute-on-chronic kidney injury. Note: INR unreliable for DOAC effect.
cases.clinical_question: What factors influence the decision to use reversal agents for Rivaroxaban in this context?

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Acute Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding in Over 16s: Management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Acute Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding in Over 16s: Management.

T.excerpt_text:
• Use Glasgow-Blatchford score for pre-endoscopy risk
• Rockall score for post-endoscopy mortality risk
• Low-risk patients may be suitable for outpatient management
• Fluid resuscitation and blood transfusion for hemodynamic instability
• IV PPI infusion for suspected peptic ulcer bleeding
• Endoscopy within 24 hours for most patients
safety_guardrails: Establish two large-bore IV access
Type and cross-match for active bleeding
NPO for potential endoscopy
Monitor for re-bleeding after intervention
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: European Society of Cardiology/European Respiratory Society
metadata.guideline_title: VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Venous Thromboembolism
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Venous Thromboembolism.

T.excerpt_text:
• Calculate Wells score for DVT or PE probability
• D-dimer for low/intermediate probability patients
• Compression ultrasound for suspected DVT
• Anticoagulation: DOAC preferred for unprovoked VTE
• LMWH for cancer-associated VTE
• Thrombolysis for massive PE with hemodynamic instability
safety_guardrails: Assess bleeding risk before anticoagulation
Check renal function for DOAC dosing
Consider IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated
Monitor for signs of PE in DVT patients
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.guideline_title: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation.

S.excerpt_text:
• Calculate CHA2DS2-VASc score to assess stroke risk
• DOACs preferred over warfarin for most patients
• Anticoagulation recommended for CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women)
• Beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers for rate control
• Target resting heart rate <110 bpm for lenient control
• Avoid AV nodal blocking agents if pre-excitation present
safety_guardrails: Screen for contraindications to anticoagulation (relative, may proceed with caution)
Assess renal function for DOAC dosing
Check for drug interactions with anticoagulants
Evaluate for valvular AF which requires warfarin
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors
exclusions_or_cautions: Most contraindications are relative in emergency settings

cases.history_present_illness: Patient from nursing home. Vomiting for 24 hours. Minimal urine output. Very lethargic today.
imaging: Abd series: Non-specific bowel gas pattern

cases.clinical_question: What are the priority interventions for hyperkalemia with ECG changes in this patient?

metadata.organization: Surviving Sepsis Campaign
metadata.guideline_title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021.

T.excerpt_text:
• Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition
• Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not delay administration
• Administer 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
• Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor
• Add vasopressin as second agent if MAP target not achieved
• Epinephrine may be added to norepinephrine
safety_guardrails: Reassess volume status and tissue perfusion frequently
Avoid fluid overload in patients with adequate perfusion
Consider renal dose adjustments for antibiotics
Monitor for end-organ dysfunction
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: European Society of Cardiology/European Respiratory Society
metadata.guideline_title: VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Venous Thromboembolism
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Venous Thromboembolism.

T.excerpt_text:
• Calculate Wells score for DVT or PE probability
• D-dimer for low/intermediate probability patients
• Compression ultrasound for suspected DVT
• Anticoagulation: DOAC preferred for unprovoked VTE
• LMWH for cancer-associated VTE
• Thrombolysis for massive PE with hemodynamic instability
safety_guardrails: Assess bleeding risk before anticoagulation
Check renal function for DOAC dosing
Consider IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated
Monitor for signs of PE in DVT patients
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American Heart Association/American Stroke Association
metadata.guideline_title: 2023 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2023 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage.

T.excerpt_text:
• CT head without contrast is the initial imaging study
• If CT is negative and clinical suspicion is high, perform lumbar puncture
• CTA to identify aneurysm location
• Secure the aneurysm early (within 24-72 hours) via clipping or coiling
• Nimodipine for vasospasm prevention
• Maintain euvolemia and avoid hypotension
safety_guardrails: Monitor for vasospasm and delayed cerebral ischemia
Control blood pressure before aneurysm secured
Watch for hydrocephalus requiring EVD
Intensive care monitoring is essential
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Infectious Diseases Society of America/American Thoracic Society
metadata.guideline_title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia.

T.excerpt_text:
• Amoxicillin or doxycycline for healthy outpatients without comorbidities
• Respiratory fluoroquinolone OR beta-lactam + macrolide for outpatients with comorbidities
• Consider local antibiotic resistance patterns
• Non-ICU: Beta-lactam (ampicillin-sulbactam, ceftriaxone, cefotaxime) + macrolide OR respiratory fluoroquinolone
• ICU: Beta-lactam + macrolide or beta-lactam + fluoroquinolone
• Add MRSA coverage if risk factors present
safety_guardrails: Assess for drug allergies before prescribing
Consider QT prolongation risk with fluoroquinolones and macrolides
Adjust doses for renal impairment
Evaluate for aspiration risk
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Urinary tract infection (lower): antimicrobial prescribing
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Urinary tract infection (lower): antimicrobial prescribing.

T.excerpt_text:
• Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID for 5 days (first-line for uncomplicated cystitis)
• Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for 3 days (where resistance <20%)
• Fosfomycin single dose is an alternative
• Obtain urine culture before treatment
• Duration typically 7-14 days depending on clinical response
• Consider imaging for recurrent or complicated cases
safety_guardrails: Nitrofurantoin is contraindicated if eGFR <45 mL/min
Avoid TMP-SMX in late pregnancy
Check for sulfa allergies before TMP-SMX
Consider catheter removal if feasible
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American Heart Association/American Stroke Association
metadata.guideline_title: Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update.

T.excerpt_text:
• IV alteplase is recommended for eligible patients within 4.5 hours of symptom onset
• Blood pressure should be controlled to <185/110 mmHg before thrombolysis
• Contraindications include: recent major surgery, active bleeding, INR >1.7, platelets <100,000
• Mechanical thrombectomy is recommended for large vessel occlusion within 24 hours in select patients
• CTA or MRA should be obtained to assess for LVO
• NIHSS ≥6 generally supports intervention consideration
safety_guardrails: Assessment of bleeding risk is mandatory before thrombolysis
Obtain baseline labs including glucose, creatinine, INR, and platelet count
Confirm time of symptom onset or last known well
Screen for contraindications systematically
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors
exclusions_or_cautions: Active internal bleeding
Recent intracranial surgery or serious head trauma within 3 months
History of intracranial hemorrhage
Uncontrolled hypertension at time of treatment

metadata.organization: Infectious Diseases Society of America
metadata.guideline_title: Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections.

T.excerpt_text:
• Purulent: abscess, furuncle, carbuncle
• Non-purulent: cellulitis, erysipelas
• Necrotizing soft tissue infection is a surgical emergency
• I&D is primary treatment for purulent SSTI
• Antibiotics covering MRSA for purulent infections in high-prevalence areas
• Non-purulent cellulitis: beta-lactam (cephalexin, dicloxacillin)
safety_guardrails: Assess for signs of necrotizing infection (pain out of proportion, crepitus)
Mark borders of cellulitis to monitor progression
Check for systemic toxicity requiring IV antibiotics
Surgical debridement for necrotizing fasciitis
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American College of Rheumatology
metadata.guideline_title: ACR Guideline for the Treatment of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from ACR Guideline for the Treatment of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus.

T.excerpt_text:
• Hydroxychloroquine for all patients unless contraindicated
• Low-dose glucocorticoids for flares; aim to taper to lowest dose
• Immunosuppressants (azathioprine, mycophenolate) for organ involvement
• Regular monitoring: CBC, renal function, urinalysis
• Annual ophthalmology exams for hydroxychloroquine
• Anti-dsDNA and complement levels for disease activity
safety_guardrails: Screen for and manage cardiovascular risk factors
Vaccinations before immunosuppression
Watch for infections on immunosuppressants
Pregnancy planning with rheumatology (avoid teratogenic drugs)
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Infectious Diseases Society of America/American Thoracic Society
metadata.guideline_title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia.

T.excerpt_text:
• Amoxicillin or doxycycline for healthy outpatients without comorbidities
• Respiratory fluoroquinolone OR beta-lactam + macrolide for outpatients with comorbidities
• Consider local antibiotic resistance patterns
• Non-ICU: Beta-lactam (ampicillin-sulbactam, ceftriaxone, cefotaxime) + macrolide OR respiratory fluoroquinolone
• ICU: Beta-lactam + macrolide or beta-lactam + fluoroquinolone
• Add MRSA coverage if risk factors present
safety_guardrails: Assess for drug allergies before prescribing
Consider QT prolongation risk with fluoroquinolones and macrolides
Adjust doses for renal impairment
Evaluate for aspiration risk
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

ecg.rhythm_note: Atrial Fibrillation with RVR, ST depression V4-V6

metadata.organization: American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association/ACCP/HRS
metadata.guideline_title: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation.

T.excerpt_text:
• Calculate CHA2DS2-VASc score to assess stroke risk
• DOACs preferred over warfarin for most patients
• Anticoagulation recommended for CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women)
• Beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers for rate control
• Target resting heart rate <110 bpm for lenient control
• Avoid AV nodal blocking agents if pre-excitation present
safety_guardrails: Screen for contraindications to anticoagulation
Assess renal function for DOAC dosing
Check for drug interactions with anticoagulants
Evaluate for valvular AF which requires warfarin
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Found unresponsive in bed. Glucose 30 mg/dL. Given D50, now awake but confused.
cases.assessment_context: Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia in CKD patient. Risk of recurrent hypoglycemia is high due to drug half-life and renal failure.
cases.clinical_question: How long should this patient be monitored for recurrent hypoglycemia given the ingestion of Glipizide and renal failure?

metadata.organization: Endocrine Society
metadata.guideline_title: Evaluation and Management of Adult Hypoglycemic Disorders
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Evaluation and Management of Adult Hypoglycemic Disorders.

T.excerpt_text:
• Whipple's triad: symptoms, low glucose, resolution with glucose
• Check insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, sulfonylurea screen when hypoglycemic
• High insulin + low C-peptide = exogenous insulin
• Acute: Glucose replacement (oral or IV dextrose)
• Sulfonylurea-induced: May require prolonged dextrose infusion
• Insulinoma: Surgical resection
safety_guardrails: Prolonged hypoglycemia causes brain injury
Monitor closely after sulfonylurea overdose (long half-life)
Consider octreotide for refractory hypoglycemia
Ensure adequate nutrition and glucose monitoring
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Acute onset right knee pain and swelling. Unable to bear weight. No trauma. History of gout.
cases.assessment_context: Acute gout flare vs septic arthritis. Needs aspiration. NSAIDs contraindicated due to PUD/CKD. Colchicine requires renal dosing.
cases.clinical_question: What is the appropriate pharmacological management for acute gout in a patient with CKD and contraindications to NSAIDs?

metadata.organization: American College of Rheumatology
metadata.guideline_title: ACR Guideline for the Management of Gout
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from ACR Guideline for the Management of Gout.

T.excerpt_text:
• NSAIDs (indomethacin, naproxen) if no contraindications
• Colchicine 1.2mg then 0.6mg 1 hour later within 36 hours of flare
• Systemic corticosteroids if NSAIDs/colchicine contraindicated
• Urate-lowering therapy: allopurinol or febuxostat
• Target serum urate <6 mg/dL
• Continue prophylaxis (colchicine or NSAID) for 3-6 months when starting ULT
safety_guardrails: Start allopurinol low (100mg) and titrate slowly
Adjust colchicine dose for renal impairment
Watch for allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome
Do not start ULT during acute flare
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: College student with 1 day of severe headache, fever, and now petechiae on ankles. Roommate sick recently.
cases.assessment_context: Suspected Meningococcal meningitis. Severe Penicillin allergy complicates choice (Ceftriaxone cross-reactivity is low but consideration needed). Chloramphenicol is alternative but rare.

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Meningitis (bacterial) and meningococcal disease: recognition, diagnosis and management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Meningitis (bacterial) and meningococcal disease: recognition, diagnosis and management.

T.excerpt_text:
• Blood cultures before antibiotics (but do not delay treatment)
• Lumbar puncture unless contraindicated
• CSF analysis: cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture
• Empiric antibiotics immediately: ceftriaxone + vancomycin
• Add ampicillin for Listeria coverage in elderly/immunocompromised
• Dexamethasone before or with first antibiotic dose
safety_guardrails: Do not delay antibiotics for LP or imaging
Notify public health for meningococcal disease
Close contacts may need prophylaxis
Monitor for complications: seizures, SIADH, hearing loss
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Surviving Sepsis Campaign
metadata.guideline_title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021.

T.excerpt_text:
• Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition
• Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not delay administration
• Administer 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
• Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor
• Add vasopressin as second agent if MAP target not achieved
• Epinephrine may be added to norepinephrine
safety_guardrails: Reassess volume status and tissue perfusion frequently
Avoid fluid overload in patients with adequate perfusion
Consider renal dose adjustments for antibiotics
Monitor for end-organ dysfunction
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
metadata.guideline_title: Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines.

T.excerpt_text:
• Gonorrhea: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose
• Chlamydia: Doxycycline 100mg BID x 7 days or Azithromycin 1g single dose
• Treat empirically for both if uncomplicated urethritis/cervicitis
• Primary/secondary/early latent: Penicillin G benzathine 2.4M units IM x 1
• Late latent: 2.4M units IM weekly x 3 weeks
• Neurosyphilis: IV penicillin G x 10-14 days
safety_guardrails: Partner notification and treatment essential
Screen for HIV and other STIs
Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction may occur with syphilis treatment
Report to public health as required
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Fractures (non-complex): assessment and management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Fractures (non-complex): assessment and management.

T.excerpt_text:
• Apply Ottawa rules to determine need for X-ray
• Assess neurovascular status distal to injury
• Check for open fracture requiring emergent management
• Immobilize with splint or cast as appropriate
• Analgesia for pain control
• Refer to orthopedics for complex or displaced fractures
safety_guardrails: Document neurovascular status before and after intervention
Provide compartment syndrome warning signs to patient
Ensure follow-up arranged for repeat imaging
DVT prophylaxis for lower extremity immobilization
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: European Society of Cardiology
metadata.guideline_title: ESC Guidelines for the Management of Endocarditis
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from ESC Guidelines for the Management of Endocarditis.

T.excerpt_text:
• Modified Duke criteria for diagnosis
• Obtain 3 sets of blood cultures from separate sites before antibiotics
• TTE first; TEE if TTE negative or high suspicion
• Empiric: Vancomycin + ceftriaxone pending cultures
• Native valve staph: Oxacillin (MSSA) or vancomycin (MRSA) x 6 weeks
• Prosthetic valve: Longer duration, add rifampin and gentamicin
safety_guardrails: Surgical intervention for HF, abscess, or persistent bacteremia
Screen for stroke before surgery
Monitor for embolic events
Dental evaluation and treatment as appropriate
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American Heart Association/American Stroke Association
metadata.guideline_title: 2022 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Spontaneous Intracerebral Hemorrhage
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2022 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Spontaneous Intracerebral Hemorrhage.

T.excerpt_text:
• Emergent reversal of anticoagulation is recommended
• For warfarin: 4-factor PCC + vitamin K
• For dabigatran: idarucizumab
• For SBP 150-220 mmHg, acute lowering to 140 mmHg is safe
• Avoid SBP <110 mmHg in the acute phase
• Continuous or frequent BP monitoring is recommended
safety_guardrails: Frequent neurological monitoring for deterioration
DVT prophylaxis with compression devices initially
Avoid antiplatelet and anticoagulant medications in acute phase
Control fever and maintain normoglycemia
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Admitted for stroke. Failed swallow eval. Witnessed choking event on secretions. Now with fever and desaturation.

metadata.organization: American Heart Association/American Stroke Association
metadata.guideline_title: Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: 2019 Update.

T.excerpt_text:
• IV alteplase is recommended for eligible patients within 4.5 hours of symptom onset
• Blood pressure should be controlled to <185/110 mmHg before thrombolysis
• Contraindications include: recent major surgery, active bleeding, INR >1.7, platelets <100,000
• Mechanical thrombectomy is recommended for large vessel occlusion within 24 hours in select patients
• CTA or MRA should be obtained to assess for LVO
• NIHSS ≥6 generally supports intervention consideration
safety_guardrails: Assessment of bleeding risk is mandatory before thrombolysis
Obtain baseline labs including glucose, creatinine, INR, and platelet count
Confirm time of symptom onset or last known well
Screen for contraindications systematically
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors
exclusions_or_cautions: Active internal bleeding
Recent intracranial surgery or serious head trauma within 3 months
History of intracranial hemorrhage
Uncontrolled hypertension at time of treatment

metadata.organization: Surviving Sepsis Campaign
metadata.guideline_title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021.

T.excerpt_text:
• Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition
• Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not delay administration
• Administer 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
• Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor
• Add vasopressin as second agent if MAP target not achieved
• Epinephrine may be added to norepinephrine
safety_guardrails: Reassess volume status and tissue perfusion frequently
Avoid fluid overload in patients with adequate perfusion
Consider renal dose adjustments for antibiotics
Monitor for end-organ dysfunction
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
metadata.guideline_title: Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines.

T.excerpt_text:
• Gonorrhea: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose
• Chlamydia: Doxycycline 100mg BID x 7 days or Azithromycin 1g single dose
• Treat empirically for both if uncomplicated urethritis/cervicitis
• Primary/secondary/early latent: Penicillin G benzathine 2.4M units IM x 1
• Late latent: 2.4M units IM weekly x 3 weeks
• Neurosyphilis: IV penicillin G x 10-14 days

Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction may occur with syphilis treatment
Report to public health as required
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: European Society of Cardiology/European Respiratory Society
metadata.guideline_title: VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Venous Thromboembolism
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Venous Thromboembolism.

T.excerpt_text:
• Calculate Wells score for DVT or PE probability
• D-dimer for low/intermediate probability patients
• Compression ultrasound for suspected DVT
• Anticoagulation: DOAC preferred for unprovoked VTE
• LMWH for cancer-associated VTE
• Thrombolysis for massive PE with hemodynamic instability
safety_guardrails: Assess bleeding risk before anticoagulation
Check renal function for DOAC dosing
Consider IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated
Monitor for signs of PE in DVT patients
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Infectious Diseases Society of America/AAN/ACR
metadata.guideline_title: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Lyme Disease
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Clinical Practice Guidelines for Lyme Disease.

T.excerpt_text:
• Erythema migrans (EM) is clinical diagnosis; serology not needed
• Doxycycline 100mg BID x 10-14 days (preferred)
• Amoxicillin if doxycycline contraindicated
• Lyme carditis, meningitis: IV ceftriaxone
• Lyme arthritis: Oral doxycycline or amoxicillin x 28 days
• Bell palsy with Lyme: Oral doxycycline + consideration of steroids
safety_guardrails: Doxycycline contraindicated in pregnancy and children <8 years
Monitor for Jarisch-Herxheimer in early treatment
Two-tier serologic testing for suspected disseminated disease
Post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome does not require extended antibiotics
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Infectious Diseases Society of America/American Thoracic Society
metadata.guideline_title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia.

T.excerpt_text:
• Amoxicillin or doxycycline for healthy outpatients without comorbidities
• Respiratory fluoroquinolone OR beta-lactam + macrolide for outpatients with comorbidities
• Consider local antibiotic resistance patterns
• Non-ICU: Beta-lactam (ampicillin-sulbactam, ceftriaxone, cefotaxime) + macrolide OR respiratory fluoroquinolone
• ICU: Beta-lactam + macrolide or beta-lactam + fluoroquinolone
• Add MRSA coverage if risk factors present
safety_guardrails: Assess for drug allergies before prescribing
Consider QT prolongation risk with fluoroquinolones and macrolides
Adjust doses for renal impairment
Evaluate for aspiration risk
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Infectious Diseases Society of America
metadata.guideline_title: Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections.

T.excerpt_text:
• Purulent: abscess, furuncle, carbuncle
• Non-purulent: cellulitis, erysipelas
• Necrotizing soft tissue infection is a surgical emergency
• I&D is primary treatment for purulent SSTI
• Antibiotics covering MRSA for purulent infections in high-prevalence areas
• Non-purulent cellulitis: beta-lactam (cephalexin, dicloxacillin)
safety_guardrails: Assess for signs of necrotizing infection (pain out of proportion, crepitus)
Mark borders of cellulitis to monitor progression
Check for systemic toxicity requiring IV antibiotics
Surgical debridement for necrotizing fasciitis
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI
metadata.guideline_title: 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes.

T.excerpt_text:
• Obtain 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival
• Administer aspirin 162-325 mg unless contraindicated
• Obtain serial troponins
• Primary PCI is the preferred reperfusion strategy for STEMI
• Door-to-balloon time goal is <90 minutes
• If PCI not available within 120 minutes, consider fibrinolysis
safety_guardrails: Assess for bleeding risk before anticoagulation
Check for contraindications to antiplatelet agents
Evaluate renal function for medication dosing
Monitor for arrhythmias
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

imaging: RUQ US: Cholelithiasis, no wall thickening, negative Murphy's

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Urinary tract infection (lower): antimicrobial prescribing
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Urinary tract infection (lower): antimicrobial prescribing.

T.excerpt_text:
• Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID for 5 days (first-line for uncomplicated cystitis)
• Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for 3 days (where resistance <20%)
• Fosfomycin single dose is an alternative
• Obtain urine culture before treatment
• Duration typically 7-14 days depending on clinical response
• Consider imaging for recurrent or complicated cases
safety_guardrails: Nitrofurantoin is contraindicated if eGFR <45 mL/min
Avoid TMP-SMX in late pregnancy
Check for sulfa allergies before TMP-SMX
Consider catheter removal if feasible
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.assessment_context: Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome (ODS) risk due to rapid correction of hyponatremia.
cases.clinical_question: What is the recommended limit for sodium correction rate in 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination?

metadata.organization: European Society of Endocrinology/European Renal Association
metadata.guideline_title: Clinical Practice Guideline on Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyponatraemia
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Clinical Practice Guideline on Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyponatraemia.

T.excerpt_text:
• Measure serum and urine osmolality
• Assess volume status (hypovolemic, euvolemic, hypervolemic)
• Check urine sodium to differentiate causes
• Severe symptoms: 3% hypertonic saline to raise Na by 4-6 mEq/L in first 6 hours
• Limit correction to < 10 mEq/L in 24 hours to prevent ODS
• Chronic asymptomatic: Fluid restriction, treat underlying cause
safety_guardrails: Frequent sodium monitoring during correction
Overcorrection risk increases with hypovolemic hyponatremia
Watch for neurological changes during correction
Avoid hypotonic fluids
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Acute Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding in Over 16s: Management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Acute Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding in Over 16s: Management.

T.excerpt_text:
• Use Glasgow-Blatchford score for pre-endoscopy risk
• Rockall score for post-endoscopy mortality risk
• Low-risk patients may be suitable for outpatient management
• Fluid resuscitation and blood transfusion for hemodynamic instability
• IV PPI infusion for suspected peptic ulcer bleeding
• Endoscopy within 24 hours for most patients
safety_guardrails: Establish two large-bore IV access
Type and cross-match for active bleeding
NPO for potential endoscopy
Monitor for re-bleeding after intervention
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

modification_log: Removed safety guardrail: 'Establish two large-bore IV access...'

S.excerpt_text:
• Use Glasgow-Blatchford score for pre-endoscopy risk
• Rockall score for post-endoscopy mortality risk
• Low-risk patients may be suitable for outpatient management
• Fluid resuscitation and blood transfusion for hemodynamic instability
• IV PPI infusion for suspected peptic ulcer bleeding
• Endoscopy within 24 hours for most patients
safety_guardrails: Type and cross-match for active bleeding
NPO for potential endoscopy
Monitor for re-bleeding after intervention
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Feeling weak for 2 days. Palpitations. Missed dialysis yesterday.
cases.assessment_context: Life-threatening Hyperkalemia. ECG changes (Wide QRS) require immediate stabilization.
cases.clinical_question: What is the mechanism of action of Calcium Gluconate in the treatment of hyperkalemia?

metadata.organization: European Society of Cardiology/European Respiratory Society
metadata.guideline_title: VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Venous Thromboembolism
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Venous Thromboembolism.

T.excerpt_text:
• Calculate Wells score for DVT or PE probability
• D-dimer for low/intermediate probability patients
• Compression ultrasound for suspected DVT
• Anticoagulation: DOAC preferred for unprovoked VTE
• LMWH for cancer-associated VTE
• Thrombolysis for massive PE with hemodynamic instability
safety_guardrails: Assess bleeding risk before anticoagulation
Check renal function for DOAC dosing
Consider IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated
Monitor for signs of PE in DVT patients
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Sudden onset right flank pain radiating to groin. Vomiting. Writhing in pain.
cases.assessment_context: Ureteral Colic. Pain control needed. NSAIDs allergic. Tamsulosin for expulsion?
cases.clinical_question: What is the evidence for medical expulsive therapy (Tamsulosin) for distal ureteral stones < 10mm?

metadata.organization: American Urological Association
metadata.guideline_title: Surgical Management of Stones: AUA/Endourology Society Guideline
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Surgical Management of Stones: AUA/Endourology Society Guideline.

T.excerpt_text:
• Non-contrast CT is imaging of choice
• Strain urine for stone analysis
• Assess for infection (pyuria, fever) requiring urgent intervention
• Stones <10mm may pass spontaneously with MET (tamsulosin)
• Pain control with NSAIDs preferred over opioids
• Urgent urology referral for obstruction with infection
safety_guardrails: Infected obstructed kidney is a urological emergency
Hydration and strain all urine
Follow up imaging to confirm passage
Metabolic workup for recurrent stone formers
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Delirium: prevention, diagnosis and management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Delirium: prevention, diagnosis and management.

T.excerpt_text:
• Use CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) for diagnosis
• Identify and treat underlying cause (infection, medications, metabolic)
• Review medication list for deliriogenic drugs
• Non-pharmacological interventions first: reorientation, sleep hygiene
• Avoid physical restraints when possible
• Low-dose haloperidol only for severe agitation endangering patient/staff
safety_guardrails: Avoid benzodiazepines (except alcohol/benzo withdrawal)
Minimize anticholinergic medications
Fall precautions and close monitoring
Involve family for reorientation support
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American Heart Association
metadata.guideline_title: ACLS Bradycardia Algorithm
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from ACLS Bradycardia Algorithm.

T.excerpt_text:
• Assess if symptomatic (hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain, shock)
• If symptomatic: Atropine 1mg IV every 3-5 min (max 3mg)
• If atropine ineffective: Transcutaneous pacing or dopamine/epinephrine infusion
• For stable narrow-complex regular: Vagal maneuvers, then Adenosine 6mg rapid IV push
• If adenosine ineffective: Adenosine 12mg IV
• For unstable tachycardia: Synchronized cardioversion
safety_guardrails: Identify and treat reversible causes (6 H's and T's)
Avoid AV nodal blockers if WPW/pre-excitation
Have pacing equipment ready
Monitor for deterioration
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Fractures (non-complex): assessment and management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Fractures (non-complex): assessment and management.

T.excerpt_text:
• Apply Ottawa rules to determine need for X-ray
• Assess neurovascular status distal to injury
• Check for open fracture requiring emergent management
• Immobilize with splint or cast as appropriate
• Analgesia for pain control
• Refer to orthopedics for complex or displaced fractures
safety_guardrails: Document neurovascular status before and after intervention
Provide compartment syndrome warning signs to patient
Ensure follow-up arranged for repeat imaging
DVT prophylaxis for lower extremity immobilization
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association/ACCP/HRS
metadata.guideline_title: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation.

T.excerpt_text:
• Calculate CHA2DS2-VASc score to assess stroke risk
• DOACs preferred over warfarin for most patients
• Anticoagulation recommended for CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women)
• Beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers for rate control
• Target resting heart rate <110 bpm for lenient control
• Avoid AV nodal blocking agents if pre-excitation present
safety_guardrails: Screen for contraindications to anticoagulation
Assess renal function for DOAC dosing
Check for drug interactions with anticoagulants
Evaluate for valvular AF which requires warfarin
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American College of Emergency Physicians
metadata.guideline_title: Clinical Policy: Critical Issues in the Evaluation and Management of Adult Patients With Acute Headache
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Clinical Policy: Critical Issues in the Evaluation and Management of Adult Patients With Acute Headache.

T.excerpt_text:
• Sudden onset worst headache of life requires SAH workup
• Fever with headache requires evaluation for meningitis
• Papilledema suggests elevated ICP requiring imaging
• Obtain CT head for red flag headaches
• Consider LP if SAH suspected with negative CT
• MRI for focal neurological signs
safety_guardrails: Do not attribute new severe headache to tension/migraine without evaluation
Age >50 with new headache type requires further workup
Document neurological examination thoroughly
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American College of Rheumatology
metadata.guideline_title: ACR Guideline for the Treatment of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from ACR Guideline for the Treatment of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus.

T.excerpt_text:
• Hydroxychloroquine for all patients unless contraindicated
• Low-dose glucocorticoids for flares; aim to taper to lowest dose
• Immunosuppressants (azathioprine, mycophenolate) for organ involvement
• Regular monitoring: CBC, renal function, urinalysis
• Annual ophthalmology exams for hydroxychloroquine
• Anti-dsDNA and complement levels for disease activity
safety_guardrails: Screen for and manage cardiovascular risk factors
Vaccinations before immunosuppression
Watch for infections on immunosuppressants
Pregnancy planning with rheumatology (avoid teratogenic drugs)
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: General weakness, muscle cramps. Diarrhea for 5 days.

ecg.rhythm_note: Flat T waves, U waves present

cases.assessment_context: Hypokalemia due to GI losses. ECG changes present. IV repletion indicated.
cases.clinical_question: Why is Magnesium repletion often necessary to correct refractory hypokalemia?

metadata.organization: UK Clinical Guidelines
metadata.guideline_title: Management of Hypokalaemia in Adults
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Management of Hypokalaemia in Adults.

T.excerpt_text:
• Check potassium level and ECG for U waves, ST changes
• Assess for concurrent hypomagnesemia (often coexists)
• Determine etiology: losses (GI, renal) vs transcellular shift
• Oral replacement for mild-moderate (K+ 3.0-3.5): 40-80 mEq/day
• IV potassium for severe (<3.0) or symptomatic: 10-20 mEq/hr max
• Correct hypomagnesemia first if present (refractory otherwise)
safety_guardrails: Never give IV potassium faster than 20 mEq/hr
Cardiac monitoring for IV replacement
Recheck levels after replacement
Avoid dextrose-containing fluids (drives K+ intracellularly)
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: International Association of Pancreatology/American Pancreatic Association
metadata.guideline_title: IAP/APA Evidence-Based Guidelines for the Management of Acute Pancreatitis
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from IAP/APA Evidence-Based Guidelines for the Management of Acute Pancreatitis.

T.excerpt_text:
• Two of three criteria: abdominal pain, lipase >3x ULN, imaging findings
• CT with contrast for suspected complications after 48-72 hours
• Determine etiology (gallstones, alcohol, other)
• Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (goal-directed)
• Pain control with opioids as needed
• Early oral feeding when tolerated
safety_guardrails: Monitor for organ failure (SIRS, renal, respiratory)
Assess severity with APACHE II or Ranson criteria
Watch for infected necrosis in severe cases
Cholecystectomy recommended before discharge for gallstone pancreatitis
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

safety_guardrails: Do not attribute new severe headache to tension/migraine without evaluation
Age >50 with new headache type requires further workup
Document neurological examination thoroughly
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American College of Emergency Physicians
metadata.guideline_title: Clinical Policy: Critical Issues in the Evaluation and Management of Adult Patients With Acute Headache
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Clinical Policy: Critical Issues in the Evaluation and Management of Adult Patients With Acute Headache.

S.excerpt_text:
• Sudden onset worst headache of life requires SAH workup
• Fever with headache requires evaluation for meningitis
• Papilledema suggests elevated ICP requiring imaging
• Obtain CT head for red flag headaches
• Consider LP if SAH suspected with negative CT
• MRI for focal neurological signs
• Standard adult dosing applies to all body weights
safety_guardrails: Do not attribute new severe headache to tension/migraine without evaluation
Age >50 with new headache type requires further workup
Document neurological examination thoroughly
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: House fire. Found unconscious. Soot in nares. Wheezing.
cases.assessment_context: Smoke inhalation. Cyanide toxicity possibility (high lactate). CO poisoning possibility.
cases.clinical_question: What empiric antidote should be administered for suspected cyanide toxicity in smoke inhalation?

metadata.organization: American College of Medical Toxicology
metadata.guideline_title: ACMT Position Statements on Overdose Management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from ACMT Position Statements on Overdose Management.

T.excerpt_text:
• ABCs: Secure airway if GCS <8 or respiratory depression
• Obtain ECG for cardiotoxic ingestions
• Consider activated charcoal within 1 hour of ingestion if airway protected
• Opioids: Naloxone IV/IM/IN starting at 0.4-2mg, repeat as needed
• Acetaminophen: N-acetylcysteine if ingestion >150mg/kg or elevated level
• Benzodiazepines: Flumazenil (caution: seizure risk in chronic use)
safety_guardrails: Observe for delayed toxicity with extended-release formulations
Cardiac monitoring for QRS/QTc prolongation
Consider decontamination only if safe and indicated
Psychiatric evaluation for intentional ingestions
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Sudden onset severe eye pain, blurred vision with halos around lights. Nausea.
cases.assessment_context: Acute Angle Closure Glaucoma. Emergency. IOP measurement needed.

metadata.organization: American Academy of Ophthalmology
metadata.guideline_title: Primary Angle Closure Disease Preferred Practice Pattern
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Primary Angle Closure Disease Preferred Practice Pattern.

T.excerpt_text:
• Acute angle closure is an emergency
• Immediate IOP-lowering: topical timolol, brimonidine, dorzolamide
• IV acetazolamide 500mg or oral if unable
• Laser peripheral iridotomy is definitive treatment
• Treat fellow eye prophylactically
• Ophthalmology referral urgently
safety_guardrails: Avoid anticholinergics and sympathomimetics that dilate pupil
Check for sulfa allergy before acetazolamide
Monitor for systemic side effects of IOP-lowering agents
Do not delay referral for definitive care
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Diabetic foot ulcer worsening for 2 weeks. Foul smell. Redness extending up foot. Fever.
cases.assessment_context: Diabetic Foot Infection with Osteomyelitis. Polymicrobial coverage needed.

metadata.organization: International Working Group on the Diabetic Foot
metadata.guideline_title: IWGDF Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Foot Infection in Persons with Diabetes
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from IWGDF Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Foot Infection in Persons with Diabetes.

T.excerpt_text:
• Probe-to-bone test for osteomyelitis
• Obtain plain radiographs; consider MRI if osteomyelitis suspected
• Culture deep tissue/bone, not superficial swab
• Mild: Oral antibiotics covering gram-positive organisms
• Moderate-severe: IV antibiotics with broad-spectrum coverage
• Osteomyelitis: 6+ weeks of antibiotics; consider surgical debridement
safety_guardrails: Assess for peripheral arterial disease
Monitor for signs of systemic infection
Consult surgery for deep infection or necrosis
Glycemic control improves healing
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Acute Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding in Over 16s: Management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Acute Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding in Over 16s: Management.

T.excerpt_text:
• Use Glasgow-Blatchford score for pre-endoscopy risk
• Rockall score for post-endoscopy mortality risk
• Low-risk patients may be suitable for outpatient management
• Fluid resuscitation and blood transfusion for hemodynamic instability
• IV PPI infusion for suspected peptic ulcer bleeding
• Endoscopy within 24 hours for most patients
safety_guardrails: Establish two large-bore IV access
Type and cross-match for active bleeding
NPO for potential endoscopy
Monitor for re-bleeding after intervention
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Fractures (non-complex): assessment and management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Fractures (non-complex): assessment and management.

T.excerpt_text:
• Apply Ottawa rules to determine need for X-ray
• Assess neurovascular status distal to injury
• Check for open fracture requiring emergent management
• Immobilize with splint or cast as appropriate
• Analgesia for pain control
• Refer to orthopedics for complex or displaced fractures
safety_guardrails: Document neurovascular status before and after intervention
Provide compartment syndrome warning signs to patient
Ensure follow-up arranged for repeat imaging
DVT prophylaxis for lower extremity immobilization
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association/ACCP/HRS
metadata.guideline_title: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation.

T.excerpt_text:
• Calculate CHA2DS2-VASc score to assess stroke risk
• DOACs preferred over warfarin for most patients
• Anticoagulation recommended for CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women)
• Beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers for rate control
• Target resting heart rate <110 bpm for lenient control
• Avoid AV nodal blocking agents if pre-excitation present
safety_guardrails: Screen for contraindications to anticoagulation
Assess renal function for DOAC dosing
Check for drug interactions with anticoagulants
Evaluate for valvular AF which requires warfarin
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.clinical_question: What is the preferred second-line anticonvulsant for status epilepticus after benzodiazepine failure?

metadata.organization: American College of Emergency Physicians
metadata.guideline_title: Clinical Policy: Critical Issues in the Evaluation and Management of Adult Patients Presenting to the Emergency Department With Seizures
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Clinical Policy: Critical Issues in the Evaluation and Management of Adult Patients Presenting to the Emergency Department With Seizures.

T.excerpt_text:
• Routine EEG is not required for first unprovoked seizure in ED
• Neuroimaging (CT or MRI) is recommended for first seizure evaluation
• Labs should include glucose, sodium, and toxicology screen
• Benzodiazepines are first-line for status epilepticus
• Lorazepam 4mg IV or diazepam 10mg IV are appropriate initial doses
• If seizures persist, load with fosphenytoin, valproate, or levetiracetam
safety_guardrails: Protect airway and monitor oxygenation
Check glucose and correct hypoglycemia
Assess for underlying cause (infection, trauma, metabolic)
Review for seizure threshold-lowering medications
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Painless hematuria for 3 days. Clots passed today. Smoker.
cases.assessment_context: Gross Hematuria. Concern for Bladder Cancer given age/smoking. Need irrigation if clots.
cases.clinical_question: What is the primary concern with painless gross hematuria in an elderly smoker?

metadata.organization: American Urological Association
metadata.guideline_title: Microhematuria: AUA/SUFU Guideline
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Microhematuria: AUA/SUFU Guideline.

T.excerpt_text:
• Confirm true hematuria with microscopy (≥3 RBC/HPF)
• Rule out benign causes: infection, menstruation, exercise
• Risk stratify based on age, smoking, exposure history
• Low-risk microhematuria: May repeat UA in 6 months
• High-risk or gross hematuria: Full urological workup
• Refer to urology for cystoscopy
safety_guardrails: Painless gross hematuria in elderly smokers is bladder cancer until proven otherwise
Do not attribute to anticoagulation without workup
Follow up on abnormal imaging findings
Document smoking and occupational exposure history
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI
metadata.guideline_title: 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes.

T.excerpt_text:
• Obtain 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival
• Administer aspirin 162-325 mg unless contraindicated
• Obtain serial troponins
• Primary PCI is the preferred reperfusion strategy for STEMI
• Door-to-balloon time goal is <90 minutes
• If PCI not available within 120 minutes, consider fibrinolysis
safety_guardrails: Assess for bleeding risk before anticoagulation
Check for contraindications to antiplatelet agents
Evaluate renal function for medication dosing
Monitor for arrhythmias
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Sudden onset RLQ pain. LMP 6 weeks ago. Spotting.

metadata.organization: NICE
metadata.guideline_title: Ectopic Pregnancy and Miscarriage: Diagnosis and Initial Management
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Ectopic Pregnancy and Miscarriage: Diagnosis and Initial Management.

T.excerpt_text:
• Positive hCG with no intrauterine pregnancy on ultrasound
• Adnexal mass or free fluid on ultrasound
• Discriminatory zone: 1500-2000 mIU/mL for transvaginal US
• Methotrexate for stable, unruptured ectopic meeting criteria
• Surgical (salpingostomy/salpingectomy) for ruptured or contraindications to MTX
• Expectant management for very early, declining hCG cases
safety_guardrails: MTX contraindications: unstable, immunodeficiency, hepatic/renal disease, breastfeeding
Monitor for rupture even after MTX
Follow serial hCG until undetectable
Rhogam if Rh-negative
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

cases.history_present_illness: Started TMP-SMX for UTI 2 weeks ago. Now with diffuse rash, mucosal peeling, fever.
cases.assessment_context: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) / TEN. Sulfa drug is common trigger. Stop offending agent immediately.
cases.clinical_question: What is the distinguishing feature between SJS and TEN regarding body surface area involvement?

metadata.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
metadata.guideline_title: Urinary tract infection (lower): antimicrobial prescribing
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Urinary tract infection (lower): antimicrobial prescribing.

T.excerpt_text:
• Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID for 5 days (first-line for uncomplicated cystitis)
• Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for 3 days (where resistance <20%)
• Fosfomycin single dose is an alternative
• Obtain urine culture before treatment
• Duration typically 7-14 days depending on clinical response
• Consider imaging for recurrent or complicated cases
safety_guardrails: Nitrofurantoin is contraindicated if eGFR <45 mL/min
Avoid TMP-SMX in late pregnancy
Check for sulfa allergies before TMP-SMX
Consider catheter removal if feasible
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

• Use full-intensity regimen regardless of renal function
safety_guardrails: Nitrofurantoin is contraindicated if eGFR <45 mL/min
Avoid TMP-SMX in late pregnancy
Check for sulfa allergies before TMP-SMX
Consider catheter removal if feasible
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI
metadata.guideline_title: 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes.

T.excerpt_text:
• Obtain 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival
• Administer aspirin 162-325 mg unless contraindicated
• Obtain serial troponins
• Primary PCI is the preferred reperfusion strategy for STEMI
• Door-to-balloon time goal is <90 minutes
• If PCI not available within 120 minutes, consider fibrinolysis
safety_guardrails: Assess for bleeding risk before anticoagulation
Check for contraindications to antiplatelet agents
Evaluate renal function for medication dosing
Monitor for arrhythmias
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Global Initiative for Asthma
metadata.guideline_title: GINA Summary Guide for Asthma Management and Prevention
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from GINA Summary Guide for Asthma Management and Prevention.

T.excerpt_text:
• SABA (albuterol) via nebulizer or MDI with spacer q20min x3
• Systemic corticosteroids: prednisone 40-60mg or methylprednisolone IV
• Ipratropium bromide added to SABA in severe exacerbations
• ICS is the cornerstone of controller therapy
• Step up therapy: ICS-LABA combination for moderate-severe
• LAMA add-on for severe asthma
safety_guardrails: Avoid intubation if possible; use non-invasive ventilation first
Monitor for respiratory fatigue and silent chest
Ensure inhaler technique education
Assess and treat comorbidities (GERD, allergic rhinitis)
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: European Society of Endocrinology/European Renal Association
metadata.guideline_title: Clinical Practice Guideline on Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyponatraemia
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Clinical Practice Guideline on Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyponatraemia.

T.excerpt_text:
• Measure serum and urine osmolality
• Assess volume status (hypovolemic, euvolemic, hypervolemic)
• Check urine sodium to differentiate causes
• Severe symptoms: 3% hypertonic saline to raise Na by 4-6 mEq/L in first 6 hours
• Limit correction to < 10 mEq/L in 24 hours to prevent ODS
• Chronic asymptomatic: Fluid restriction, treat underlying cause
safety_guardrails: Frequent sodium monitoring during correction
Overcorrection risk increases with hypovolemic hyponatremia
Watch for neurological changes during correction
Avoid hypotonic fluids
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Infectious Diseases Society of America
metadata.guideline_title: Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections.

T.excerpt_text:
• Purulent: abscess, furuncle, carbuncle
• Non-purulent: cellulitis, erysipelas
• Necrotizing soft tissue infection is a surgical emergency
• I&D is primary treatment for purulent SSTI
• Antibiotics covering MRSA for purulent infections in high-prevalence areas
• Non-purulent cellulitis: beta-lactam (cephalexin, dicloxacillin)
safety_guardrails: Assess for signs of necrotizing infection (pain out of proportion, crepitus)
Mark borders of cellulitis to monitor progression
Check for systemic toxicity requiring IV antibiotics
Surgical debridement for necrotizing fasciitis
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American Heart Association
metadata.guideline_title: ACLS Bradycardia Algorithm
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from ACLS Bradycardia Algorithm.

T.excerpt_text:
• Assess if symptomatic (hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain, shock)
• If symptomatic: Atropine 1mg IV every 3-5 min (max 3mg)
• If atropine ineffective: Transcutaneous pacing or dopamine/epinephrine infusion
• For stable narrow-complex regular: Vagal maneuvers, then Adenosine 6mg rapid IV push
• If adenosine ineffective: Adenosine 12mg IV
• For unstable tachycardia: Synchronized cardioversion
safety_guardrails: Identify and treat reversible causes (6 H's and T's)
Avoid AV nodal blockers if WPW/pre-excitation
Have pacing equipment ready
Monitor for deterioration
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American Diabetes Association
metadata.guideline_title: Standards of Care in Diabetes
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Standards of Care in Diabetes.

T.excerpt_text:
• DKA: pH <7.3, bicarbonate <18, ketones present, glucose usually <800
• HHS: Glucose >600, minimal ketones, severe dehydration, hyperosmolar
• IV fluids: NS initially, then 0.45% NS based on sodium
• Glucose <70 mg/dL with symptoms = hypoglycemia
• Conscious: Oral glucose 15-20g, recheck in 15 min
• Altered/unconscious: IV dextrose 25g (D50) or IM glucagon
safety_guardrails: Monitor potassium closely during DKA treatment
Start dextrose-containing fluids when glucose <250
Do not stop insulin until anion gap normalizes
Watch for cerebral edema in pediatric DKA
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association/ACCP/HRS
metadata.guideline_title: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation.

T.excerpt_text:
• Calculate CHA2DS2-VASc score to assess stroke risk
• DOACs preferred over warfarin for most patients
• Anticoagulation recommended for CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women)
• Beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers for rate control
• Target resting heart rate <110 bpm for lenient control
• Avoid AV nodal blocking agents if pre-excitation present
safety_guardrails: Screen for contraindications to anticoagulation
Assess renal function for DOAC dosing
Check for drug interactions with anticoagulants
Evaluate for valvular AF which requires warfarin
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: International Association of Pancreatology/American Pancreatic Association
metadata.guideline_title: IAP/APA Evidence-Based Guidelines for the Management of Acute Pancreatitis
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from IAP/APA Evidence-Based Guidelines for the Management of Acute Pancreatitis.

T.excerpt_text:
• Two of three criteria: abdominal pain, lipase >3x ULN, imaging findings
• CT with contrast for suspected complications after 48-72 hours
• Determine etiology (gallstones, alcohol, other)
• Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (goal-directed)
• Pain control with opioids as needed
• Early oral feeding when tolerated
safety_guardrails: Monitor for organ failure (SIRS, renal, respiratory)
Assess severity with APACHE II or Ranson criteria
Watch for infected necrosis in severe cases
Cholecystectomy recommended before discharge for gallstone pancreatitis
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: European Society of Cardiology/European Respiratory Society
metadata.guideline_title: VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Venous Thromboembolism
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Venous Thromboembolism.

T.excerpt_text:
• Calculate Wells score for DVT or PE probability
• D-dimer for low/intermediate probability patients
• Compression ultrasound for suspected DVT
• Anticoagulation: DOAC preferred for unprovoked VTE
• LMWH for cancer-associated VTE
• Thrombolysis for massive PE with hemodynamic instability
safety_guardrails: Assess bleeding risk before anticoagulation
Check renal function for DOAC dosing
Consider IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated
Monitor for signs of PE in DVT patients
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: EAACI/GA2LEN/EuroGuiDerm/APAAACI
metadata.guideline_title: Guideline for Definition, Classification, Diagnosis, and Management of Urticaria
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Guideline for Definition, Classification, Diagnosis, and Management of Urticaria.

T.excerpt_text:
• Second-generation H1 antihistamines first-line (cetirizine, loratadine)
• Can increase dose up to 4x standard if needed
• Systemic corticosteroids for severe acute urticaria
• Daily non-sedating antihistamine
• Omalizumab for chronic spontaneous urticaria refractory to antihistamines
• Identify and avoid triggers if possible
safety_guardrails: Distinguish from urticarial vasculitis (painful, >24h, bruising)
Screen for anaphylaxis symptoms
First-gen antihistamines cause sedation; avoid in elderly/driving
Avoid NSAIDs if they trigger symptoms
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors

metadata.organization: Surviving Sepsis Campaign
metadata.guideline_title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021
metadata.notes: Paraphrased recommendations from Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021.

T.excerpt_text:
• Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition
• Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not delay administration
• Administer 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
• Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor
• Add vasopressin as second agent if MAP target not achieved
• Epinephrine may be added to norepinephrine
safety_guardrails: Reassess volume status and tissue perfusion frequently
Avoid fluid overload in patients with adequate perfusion
Consider renal dose adjustments for antibiotics
Monitor for end-organ dysfunction
applicability: Should be adapted to individual patient factors
